original_txt:
Russianmade ventilators catch fire kill Covidpositive patients in Moscow and St PetersburgRussianmade ventilators catch fire kill Covidpositive patients

Five people were killed in a fire that sparked in the intensive care unit at St Georges Hospital in St Petersburg which was earlier redesigned to treat patients with coronavirus

The fire occurred on the sixth floor in the intensive care unit the fire affected an area of ten square meters

All the killed patients were connected to ventilators Four of them died as a result of carbon monoxide exposure another one died when the ventilator to which the patient was connected was turned off According to preliminary reports the fire started due to the hazardous overload of the ventilator

The ventilators are operating to the maximum It is believed that the equipment was overloaded and the apparatus caught fire officials said

Reportedly there were 16 patients in the department at the moment of the accident Hospital personnel managed to move eleven of them to another wing of the building In total as many as 150 people were evacuated from the building the fire was localized All the patients and personnel who were staying in the intensive care unit during the fire were transferred to the first floor of the hospital

The Investigative Committee opened a criminal case under Part 3 of Article 109 of the Criminal Code of the Russian Federation Causing death by negligence Prosecutors currently look into compliance with fire safety requirements at the hospital

On the evening of May 9 a fire occurred in the building of the Moscow hospital No 50 named after Spasokukotsky where patients with coronavirus are also admitted The fire broke out in the intensive care unit and began to spread along the first floor having killed one woman patient with COVID19 Three others were injured

Within 30 minutes all 295 patients were evacuated It is believed that the fire started because of defective ventilators too

According to law enforcement agencies the ventilators that caused the fires at hospitals in Moscow and St Petersburg were manufactured at one factory  the Ural InstrumentMaking Plant The ventilator at the fireaffected hospital in St Petersburg was installed there on May 3

According to preliminary reports the fire at the Moscow hospital occurred as a result of short circuit which caused oxygen to catch fire Other versions include short circuit malfunction overload and improper operation
corr_txt:
Russian-made ventilators ignite, leading to fatalities among COVID-positive patients in Moscow and St. Petersburg.

Five individuals perished in a blaze that erupted on the sixth floor of St. George's Hospital in St. Petersburg, which had been repurposed for treating coronavirus patients.

The fire consumed an area spanning ten square meters within the intensive care unit.

All deceased were attached to ventilators; four succumbed to carbon monoxide poisoning while another died when their ventilator was shut off. Preliminary findings suggest that the equipment overheated due to excessive load, causing it to ignite.

Hospital staff reported that the ventilators were operating at peak capacity and became overloaded, leading to the fire. At the time of the incident, there were 16 patients in the ward; eleven were moved to a different section of the building. A total of 150 people were evacuated from the premises, with all ICU occupants relocated to the ground floor.

The Investigative Committee has initiated a criminal investigation under Part 3 of Article 109 of the Russian Federation's Criminal Code for causing death through negligence. Prosecutors are examining adherence to fire safety regulations at the hospital.

On May 9 evening, a similar incident occurred in Moscow Hospital No. 50 named after Spasokukotsky, where patients with coronavirus were also treated. A fire broke out on the first floor of its intensive care unit, resulting in one fatality and three injuries among COVID-positive patients.

Within half an hour, all 295 patients were safely evacuated. The cause is believed to be a defective ventilator that led to a short circuit igniting oxygen supplies. Other hypotheses include malfunction, overload, or improper operation of the equipment.

According to law enforcement, the faulty ventilators involved in both incidents at hospitals in Moscow and St. Petersburg were produced by the Ural Instrument-Making Plant. The ventilator installed at the affected hospital in St. Petersburg was put into use on May 3.
